# Topic 1 - Metric Spaces

## Introduction

You have been doing mathematics on the real number line and in Euclidean space since your earliest courses. When MATH 101 asked you to find the distance between two points, you reached for the Pythagorean theorem without hesitation. When MATH 213 (Analysis I) asked you whether a sequence converges, you checked whether the terms got closer and closer to some limit. In every case, the word "closer" was doing enormous work — and you never had to question what it meant, because you were always working in a familiar, flat, well-behaved space where distance was obvious.

Metric Spaces are what happen when you ask the question that nobody asked before: what is the minimal set of rules that "distance" must obey, no matter what space you are working in? Not distance on a number line, not distance in a city grid, not distance between probability distributions — but distance in the most general sense possible. If you can answer that question cleanly, then every theorem you prove about "closeness" will automatically apply to all of those spaces at once. You prove it once, and it works everywhere.

This is the leap that unlocks the rest of the roadmap. The spaces you will work with in later phases — spaces of probability distributions, spaces of functions, spaces of graphs — are not Euclidean. They are strange, high-dimensional, sometimes infinite-dimensional objects. But if you can equip them with a valid notion of distance, all the machinery of analysis applies. Open and closed sets, convergence, completeness, compactness — these are not properties of Euclidean space. They are properties of any space with a well-defined metric.

The four subtopics here — open and closed sets, compactness, completeness, and Banach and Hilbert spaces — build on each other in a precise order. Open and closed sets define the basic vocabulary of "nearness." Compactness captures the idea that a space has no escape routes. Completeness captures the idea that a space has no holes. And Banach and Hilbert spaces are the infinite-dimensional arenas where the functions and distributions of the roadmap actually live.

---
---
